title: Saving For Arraignment Day

jokes.text: The narrator's opening monologue: 'the one son who had no choice but to keep them all together'
jokes.setup: The classic Arrested Development title card framing Michael as a noble martyr holding the family together
jokes.punchline: By Season 5 the audience knows Michael is just as dysfunctional and self-serving as everyone else, making 'no choice' darkly ironic
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely contextual — it's only funny if you've watched the whole show. The running irony of Michael being framed as the competent one while being just as broken is a series-long gag that lands harder with each season.

jokes.text: Family pageant critique: 'A little more Magnum, PI, a little less Higgins'
jokes.setup: Lucille is dissatisfied with the swimsuit competition; she directs Michael to be more sexually appealing
jokes.punchline: She frames her critique as a TV reference — Magnum PI (sexy) vs. Higgins (stuffy) — applied to her own son in a swimsuit
jokes.explanation: Works as character comedy because Lucille sexualizing her son while critiquing him in a pageant she organized is perfectly in character. The Magnum/Higgins framing is specific enough to be clever without being obscure.

jokes.text: Stan Sitwell's eyebrow: 'Oh, for God sakes. Is that Stan Sitwell's eyebrow? I... I just told him he didn't leave it here!'
jokes.setup: Stan Sitwell, the Bluth family nemesis, has a condition causing him to lose hair — including eyebrows — which is a long-running visual gag across the series
jokes.punchline: Someone (presumably Michael) is wearing Stan Sitwell's lost eyebrow as a prop/costume piece, and Lucille is outraged she told him it wasn't there
jokes.explanation: A textbook AD layered callback: the visual gag of a family member wearing a hairpiece, the callback to Sitwell's condition, and Lucille's indignant denial all land simultaneously. The 'I just told him he didn't leave it here' implies she's been hiding it, which adds another layer.

jokes.text: 'I'm Gob.' / 'Uh... Why did you name him that?'
jokes.setup: The grandmother meets the adult grandchildren she hasn't seen in five years
jokes.punchline: Gob introduces himself and the grandmother's first question is why anyone would name a child 'Gob' — treating his name as an obvious absurdity
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — two lines, maximum effect. The joke works because the audience has spent five seasons accepting 'Gob' as a normal name, and an outsider's fresh reaction is the correct one. The grandmother's guileless confusion is funnier than any insult could be.

jokes.text: Lucille to her mother: 'You don't need to come in here and criticize my children in the middle of a pageant. We're...'
jokes.setup: Lucille scolds her mother for criticizing the children
jokes.punchline: Lucille — who spends every episode criticizing her children — is indignant about someone else doing it, with zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Competent character irony — the joke is that Lucille is the biggest critic of her children and has no standing to make this complaint. Functional but not particularly fresh.

jokes.text: 'You never let me wear the sash when I was little.' / 'Oh, please, you were never little.' / 'Your mother was never little.'
jokes.setup: Lucille complains she was never allowed to wear the pageant sash as a child
jokes.punchline: The grandmother dismisses this with 'you were never little' — implying Lucille was born a full-sized, imperious adult — then the narrator repeats it as objective fact
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan repetition ('Your mother was never little') is the key move — it validates the grandmother's insult as documented fact rather than a cutting remark, which is the AD narrator's signature move. Highly quotable and perfectly structured with the echo beat. Justifies 8+ because the two-part delivery (grandma's line + narrator confirmation) is a genuinely clever structural choice that couldn't work in any other format.

jokes.text: Trophy dispute: 'We already have a trophy for the winner. It has a little magician on it.' / 'They didn't have one with a boy doing push-ups.'
jokes.setup: Grandmother wants to give her own prize; Lucille says there's already a trophy
jokes.punchline: The trophy has a magician on it (implying Gob always wins), and the explanation for why it's not a different design is because they couldn't find one with a boy doing push-ups — implying Michael's talent is literally push-ups
jokes.explanation: Dense information delivery: we learn simultaneously that Gob is the presumed winner (magician trophy), that Michael's talent is something as pathetically mundane as push-ups, and that the family has been running this pageant long enough to have a house trophy. Classic AD efficiency.

jokes.text: Grandmother's prize reveal: 'It's an all-expense paid cruise... with her grandmother.'
jokes.setup: Grandmother announces a prize for whoever wins the pageant
jokes.punchline: The prize is spending time with the grandmother herself — framed as a gift, implying she doesn't recognize she's the punishment
jokes.explanation: The narrator's framing (switching to third person 'with her grandmother') does the comic work — a clean beat. The concept is a familiar 'the gift IS the torture' format but executed with the show's characteristic detachment.

jokes.text: 'You know, I've been in the house five minutes. You haven't even offered me so much as a pitcher of martinis.' / 'I'll make two.'
jokes.setup: Grandmother complains about not being offered a drink after five minutes in the house
jokes.punchline: She specifies 'a pitcher of martinis' as the minimum hospitality threshold; Lucille responds by offering to make two pitchers — entirely in character
jokes.explanation: Two-line joke with perfect economy. 'A pitcher of martinis' is already the joke (a pitcher, not a glass), and 'I'll make two' reveals that Lucille considers this a normal and reasonable amount. It's character comedy at its most efficient — both women's alcoholism and their competitive one-upmanship expressed in seven words.

jokes.text: Jack dumped Grandmother: 'He says he wants to start a clean slate on his 80th birthday.'
jokes.setup: Grandmother explains why she's not taking a cruise with Uncle Jack this year
jokes.punchline: Being dumped at (presumably) 79 by an 80-year-old who wants a 'fresh start' — the absurdity of romantic drama at extreme old age treated as mundane
jokes.explanation: Mild background gag about elderly romantic drama. The '80th birthday clean slate' is a funny concept but underdeveloped — it's played too straight to land as more than a chuckle.

jokes.text: 'My God, when he was three years old, you still had him on a leash! I said, She's gonna make a murderer out of that one.'
jokes.setup: Lucille won't let grandmother take Buster on a cruise; grandmother protests by citing Lucille's overprotection of Buster as a child
jokes.punchline: The grandmother predicted Buster would become a murderer when he was three years old — which is now literally true (Buster is on trial for murder)
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The grandmother's throwaway prediction ('she's gonna make a murderer out of that one') is both a character joke about Lucille's toxic parenting AND a literal plot spoiler confirmed by the present-day narrative. The leash detail echoes Lucille's documented suffocating control of Buster. The joke operates on three levels: dark comedy, character truth, and earned dramatic irony. Justifies 8+ because it's genuinely surprising, perfectly constructed, and does real thematic work about how Lucille's parenting shaped Buster's fate.

jokes.text: 'And I can be a judge.' / 'Never too early to get Buster used to being in front of a judge.'
jokes.setup: Grandmother volunteers to judge the pageant
jokes.punchline: The narrator reframes this as practical preparation for Buster's imminent trial — treating Buster's criminal jeopardy as a developmental milestone
jokes.explanation: The narrator's dry recontextualization is exemplary AD comedy writing. The sentence is structured like parenting advice ('never too early to...') which makes the content ('being in front of a judge for murder') even more jarring. Justifies 8+ for the structural elegance of the parenting-advice format applied to criminal proceedings.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Sr. and Lucille entered a courtroom that abounded with memories of Bluth family get-togethers.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille enter the courtroom for Buster's arraignment
jokes.punchline: The courtroom 'abounds with memories of Bluth family get-togethers' — meaning the Bluths have been in court so often it functions as a family gathering place
jokes.explanation: Solid narrator joke in the AD tradition — framing criminal proceedings as warm family nostalgia. The specific word 'abounded' is a nice formal touch that heightens the absurdity.

jokes.text: The bailiff Chuck / 'You used to catch him when he'd run.' / 'Actually, I'm Chuck's son.' / 'Your parents must be so proud.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. recognizes the bailiff as Chuck, who used to escort him during his trials; they compliment his career
jokes.punchline: It's actually Chuck's son — meaning the Bluths have been in court across multiple generations of this bailiff's family, and 'your parents must be so proud' takes on a double meaning (proud of their son, and presumably glad the Bluths keep providing work)
jokes.explanation: Efficient joke establishing the Bluths' long criminal history through a human detail. The generational bailiff is a nice touch — these families have grown up together through Bluth crimes.

jokes.text: 'Well, why don't I just chain your boy to the bench here?' / 'That would be fine. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: CJ offers to chain Buster to the courtroom bench
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s matter-of-fact 'That would be fine. Thank you.' — accepting the chaining of his son as a reasonable practical arrangement with polite gratitude
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s patrician calm in accepting his son's literal chaining is pitch-perfect character comedy. The 'Thank you' is the key word — the extra politeness makes it much funnier.

jokes.text: Lucille to George Sr.: 'At least I came to your trial. For mine, you were too busy juicing it up with that woman you probably pushed...'
jokes.setup: Buster is led into the courtroom; family tension surfaces
jokes.punchline: Lucille's complaint casually implies George Sr. pushed someone (possibly killed them) while also revealing he was drunk ('juicing it up') at her trial — layered accusations delivered as petty marital grievance
jokes.explanation: Characteristically AD — a mundane marital complaint that smuggles in dark criminal implications. 'That woman you probably pushed' is doing a lot of work: it implies murder, it's unprovable, and it's delivered as a casual accusation in an ongoing argument.

jokes.text: Buster growing dreadlocks: 'I'm growing dreads, Mother. Dreads right out of my head.' / 'Just because you have an African-American left hand, all of a sudden you're Ray Charles?'
jokes.setup: Buster has grown dreadlocks; Lucille disapproves
jokes.punchline: Lucille attributes Buster's dreadlocks to his prosthetic hook/Black left hand, and compares him to Ray Charles — who was blind, not a dreadlock-wearer — suggesting Lucille's racism and ignorance are simultaneous
jokes.explanation: Dense character joke: Lucille's racism is wrong on its own terms (Ray Charles was not known for dreadlocks), her logic is absurd (a prosthetic hand influencing hairstyle choices), and the whole exchange reveals her simultaneously othering Black people and being completely wrong about which Black celebrity she's referencing. Very specific to these characters.

jokes.text: 'And I thought you Alibabaed one that was less... threatening.'
jokes.setup: Lucille referring to Buster's prosthetic hook/hand
jokes.punchline: Using 'Alibaba' as a verb for online shopping (conflating it with eBay/Amazon) while describing shopping for a prosthetic limb by perceived racial threat level
jokes.explanation: Lucille's casual racism + tech ignorance + the absurdity of rating prosthetic limbs by 'threat level' is a densely characterful line. 'Alibabaed' as a malapropism for online shopping is a nice detail of her out-of-touch wealth.

jokes.text: Barry arrives: 'Oh, no, no, no, no. I'm just here as a fan. And for the referral fee.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Barry is their lawyer; Barry says he's not involved
jokes.punchline: He immediately undercuts 'just here as a fan' by admitting he's also collecting a referral fee — which is mercenary and also technically work
jokes.explanation: Barry's cheerful self-interest is a reliable character gag. The 'and for the referral fee' addition demolishes his claim of disinterested fandom efficiently.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Shouldn't you be working on the wall?' / George Sr.: 'Uh, you know what? I want to... I want to take a step back. I want to let my son flap his wings a bit.' / 'Oh, God. Did he take another swing at you?' / 'No, that... that's not what he did.'
jokes.setup: Barry asks why George Sr. isn't working on the wall; George gives a vague parenting-philosophy answer
jokes.punchline: Barry's immediate assumption that 'flap his wings' means George Michael physically attacked George Sr. again — normalizing assault as a Bluth family dynamic
jokes.explanation: Barry's interpretation reveals more about the family's history than George Sr.'s euphemism does. That Barry's first guess is 'he hit you again' is the damning detail.

jokes.text: ...the Guilty Guys. Very expensive but very, very good, especially if you're guilty.
jokes.setup: Barry has recommended a law firm called 'the Guilty Guys.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator deadpans 'especially if you're guilty' — the name of the firm is both their USP and their limitation, which becomes the central joke of the trial sequence.
jokes.explanation: The name 'Guilty Guys' is itself a strong comedic construction, and 'especially if you're guilty' is a perfect encapsulating punchline. It sets up the entire Guilty Guys arc efficiently.

jokes.text: Narrator recap of plot: 'They're going to be mildly displeased.' (describing Chinese investors' reaction to Michael spending their money)
jokes.setup: Narrator recaps that Michael secretly spent Chinese investors' money
jokes.punchline: 'Mildly displeased' is a massive understatement for what would clearly be a grave international financial and possibly criminal offense
jokes.explanation: The narrator's signature understatement mode. 'Mildly displeased' for what is effectively fraud against Chinese criminal investors is a reliable AD narrator gag.

jokes.text: The Guilty Guys — introduced as a law firm whose record was 'so unblemished,' they were the subject of a Netflix reality show 'abandoned early on as not being binge-worthy'
jokes.setup: The narrator introduces the Guilty Guys as Barry's recommended lawyers with a sterling reputation
jokes.punchline: Their reality show was canceled by Netflix for not being binge-worthy — a meta joke about Arrested Development's own Netflix situation
jokes.explanation: Sharp meta-commentary: the Netflix cancellation joke directly addresses AD's own complicated relationship with Netflix (the show was revived there but not considered a mainstream success). The 'not binge-worthy' phrasing is perfect because it's Netflix's own language used against them. Justifies 8+ because it's genuinely clever self-awareness that works as both a character beat AND industry commentary, and it's specific enough to reward savvy viewers without alienating casual ones.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I'm gonna go in the hall and see if they're here.' / 'I'm gonna try and get a selfie with the G-Man.'
jokes.setup: Barry offers to check on the lawyers
jokes.punchline: Barry's actual motivation is to get a celebrity selfie with a defendant in a murder trial — the 'G-Man' being Buster, his own client's family member
jokes.explanation: Barry's oblivious self-interest is a reliable character gag. 'The G-Man' for Buster is funny but the moment is minor.

jokes.text: Oh, God. Did he take another swing at you? / No, that... that's not what he did. / George Michael fired you? / That is what he did.
jokes.setup: Lucille guesses George Michael hit George Sr. — a reasonable guess given family history.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s reluctant admission 'that is what he did' after his euphemism fails is the joke — the stilted phrasing suggesting deep humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The formal phrasing 'that is what he did' after the preceding wingflapping euphemism is the joke — George Sr. forced into acknowledging the truth through the most dignified possible language. Solid character work.

jokes.text: Gob explaining the severed foot: 'Yeah. Although they call it a mum.' / 'Mum's the word for severed human foot in Chinese.'
jokes.setup: Gob told Michael the Chinese investors sent a severed foot as a threat but said 'mum's the word' about it — Michael discovers this is not the expression he thought
jokes.punchline: Gob confidently claims 'mum' is the Chinese word for severed foot — completely fabricated, delivered with total authority — and also accidentally explains why Michael was kept in the dark (George Sr. said 'mum's the word' meaning keep quiet, but Gob interpreted it as a cultural definition)
jokes.explanation: This is among the best jokes in the episode. It operates on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) the idiom 'mum's the word' used literally as a foreign-language definition, (2) Gob's absolute confidence in completely wrong information, (3) the callback to George Sr.'s instruction being hilariously misinterpreted, (4) the absurdity of 'severed human foot' being casually defined as a vocabulary term. Gob's 'I don't even know how I know that' is the perfect button — he has no idea where this fact came from because he invented it. Justifies 8+ because it's layered, character-specific, quotable, and structurally elegant.

jokes.text: Gob's monologue: 'You know what? Like, call me after you've been lured down to a rock quarry, and someone threatens to ruin your magic career if you go to gay conversion therapy, and it works.'
jokes.setup: Gob dismisses Michael's concerns about the severed foot threat as overreaction
jokes.punchline: Gob rattles off his own experiences as a comparison — lured to a quarry, magic career threatened, gay conversion therapy 'working' — delivered as a casual reference to his deeply traumatic recent experiences
jokes.explanation: Gob's rattled-off backstory callback is a tour de force of character comedy. The escalating absurdity of each phrase ('lured to a rock quarry,' 'ruin your magic career,' 'gay conversion therapy, and it works') each deserves its own joke but they're delivered as a throwaway comparison. 'And it works' is the perfect dark button — Gob converted to straight but it's played as the most traumatic part. Justifies 8+ because only Gob could deliver this speech, it earns every reference through prior seasons, and the delivery rhythm is genuinely impressive writing.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Well, having a child try to embarrass you is part of parenting, sweetheart.' / 'Mine's chained to a bench. So, in that sense, he's an overachiever.'
jokes.setup: Someone (likely about George Michael firing Michael) observes that children embarrass their parents
jokes.punchline: Lucille's child being 'chained to a bench' (for murder) is framed as the child being an 'overachiever' in the art of parental embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Among the best lines in the episode. The reframing of 'murder defendant chained in court' as a form of overachievement is Lucille at her most brilliantly sociopathic. 'In that sense' is the key phrase — she's acknowledging she's making an unusual argument and proceeding anyway. Highly quotable, perfectly structured, and entirely Lucille. Justifies 8+ because the logic is airtight within its own warped framework and the recontextualization is genuinely clever.

jokes.text: The Guilty Guys arrive and the bailiff CJ announces: 'All rise for the Guilty Guys.' / 'CJ, you don't have to...' / 'Okay, great. Now I can't see.'
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys make a dramatic entrance; CJ the bailiff calls 'All rise' for them as if they were the judge
jokes.punchline: CJ clearly got swept up in the hype of the famous lawyers; someone shorter (presumably Lucille) can't see over the standing crowd
jokes.explanation: Quick physical-comedy beat — the celebrity-worship 'all rise' and the complaint about not being able to see are both functional. 'Now I can't see' is a nice mundane complaint in an absurd context.

jokes.text: Guilty Guys plead 'guilty' for Buster
jokes.setup: The expensive, elaborate Guilty Guys finally arrive after a dramatic entrance; the court asks for a plea
jokes.punchline: They immediately and confidently plead 'guilty' — exactly as their name suggests, but to a client who is claiming innocence
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the Guilty Guys setup is perfectly structured. Their name is the punchline — they plead guilty because that's literally what they do. The extended setup of their dramatic arrival makes the anti-climactic guilty plea even funnier. Justifies 8+ for the structural elegance of the name-as-punchline and the sustained escalation before it.

jokes.text: Judge's speech interrupted: 'it is not often in the course of a jurisprudence career that a judge has the... I want to say privilege...' / 'Sorry. Did... Did they say guilty?'
jokes.setup: The judge is beginning a heartfelt speech about the Guilty Guys' prestige
jokes.punchline: Michael's delayed-processing interruption breaks the judicial moment — he only just caught what was said
jokes.explanation: The delayed-reaction beat is a reliable comedy structure. Michael processing 'guilty' three sentences late while the judge is mid-speech is a solid execution.

jokes.text: Look at that. There is always a guy who wants to know what's going on. It's why I could never go to the movies with my father.
jokes.setup: Michael interrupts to ask if the Guilty Guys just said 'guilty.'
jokes.punchline: The judge responds with a personal anecdote about not being able to go to movies with his father — completely inappropriately breaking judicial decorum to complain about Michael's confusion.
jokes.explanation: The judge breaking into a personal anecdote mid-trial is absurdist and very funny — the specificity of 'I could never go to movies with my father' suggests a whole backstory and humanizes an authority figure in the most undignified way. The leap from legal proceedings to personal grievance is unexpected and well-executed.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Look at that. There is always a guy who wants to know what's going on. It's why I could never go to the movies with my father.'
jokes.setup: Michael interrupts the proceedings to ask what's happening
jokes.punchline: The narrator breaks the fourth wall to editorialize about 'that guy who asks questions' — and then volunteers a personal anecdote about his father, the first time the narrator has been explicitly personal
jokes.explanation: The AD narrator becoming briefly personal and annoyed is a lovely meta move. The specific detail of 'my father' grounds the abstraction in a funny human complaint. The narrator stepping out of omniscient mode to be petty is very AD.

jokes.text: 'Hello? Not!' / 'We'd like to plead not guilty, Your Honor.'
jokes.setup: After the guilty plea confusion, someone (or the lawyers themselves) tries to correct
jokes.punchline: 'Hello? Not!' as a correction device — both a 90s cultural reference and the actual legal correction happening simultaneously; the 'not guilty' is delivered with the structure of the old 'psyche/not' joke
jokes.explanation: Clever structural use of the 'not' construction — it delivers the legal correction while functioning as a dated cultural joke. The layers work together even if neither is exceptional alone.

jokes.text: Guilty Guy: 'Not guilty? You hired us to plead not guilty?' / 'Well, that's a first.'
jokes.setup: The family clarifies they want a not-guilty plea
jokes.punchline: The Guilty Guys have literally never been hired to plead not guilty — their entire business model is the opposite — and treat this as a novel and baffling request
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the Guilty Guys joke. 'That's a first' is the right deadpan button — it confirms their entire operation is built on guilty clients and they've never encountered innocence (or a claim of it) before.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I thought I told you that if I was guilty, then I would hire these guys, 'cause they get the best sentences.'
jokes.setup: The family is outraged that Barry recommended lawyers who only handle guilty pleas
jokes.punchline: Barry reveals his recommendation was conditional — he only meant to hire them if the client was guilty — which is Barry-level legal logic that makes no practical sense
jokes.explanation: Barry's self-justifying non-logic is perfectly in character. He genuinely believes he explained himself clearly, and his logic is internally consistent but practically insane — a great Barry beat.

jokes.text: Guilty Guys sidebar: 'Yeah, we're out. We just can't...' / 'Really?' / 'We can't defend their son. Bye.' / 'Yes! Yes! We did it! They're out.'
jokes.setup: The family asks if the Guilty Guys will stay on for a not-guilty plea; they call a sidebar
jokes.punchline: The sidebar lasts three seconds — they immediately bail — and George Sr. celebrates as if this is a victory
jokes.explanation: The anti-climactic sidebar (described in the narration as 'anti-climactic') and George Sr.'s baffling celebration both land well. 'Bye' from the lawyer is the right curt exit.

jokes.text: Barry describes DA Lottie Dottie: 'She has never lost a case. Well, just once. But then, her ex was the opposing counsel.'
jokes.setup: Barry describes how formidable the DA is
jokes.punchline: She has 'never lost a case' immediately qualified by 'well, just once' — and the exception is explained by personal/romantic distraction, setting up the entire Dusty subplot
jokes.explanation: Efficient setup for the Dusty mistrial plot. The quick qualification of 'never lost a case' with 'well, just once' is a reliable comedy structure and here it also does plot work.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I'll let him move in. That's what he wants.' / George Sr.: 'No, no, no, no. You're so close to ending it with him. I mean, I... You don't want to be leading him on.'
jokes.setup: Lucille proposes letting Dusty move in to secure his legal representation
jokes.punchline: George Sr. warns Lucille not to 'lead him on' — the husband is advising his wife on how to manage her boyfriend with apparent sincerity and apparent concern for the boyfriend's feelings
jokes.explanation: The Bluth marital dynamic where George Sr. counsels Lucille on her boyfriend is solid character comedy. His concern about her 'leading on' her paramour is delivered with genuine spousal concern, which is the joke.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'So that's my choice? Between my wife and my son?' / 'Isn't that the worst? Especially when you're checking into a hotel.' / 'Here's my advice. Always say that he's your wife, 'cause they never have the balls to check.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. bemoans having to choose between wife and son
jokes.punchline: The narrator (or a voice) responds as if George Sr. literally meant choosing between two people for a hotel room — offering absurd practical advice about hotel check-in
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur pivot to hotel check-in advice is a nice absurdist beat. The specificity of 'they never have the balls to check' is funny — it's confident practical wisdom about a very specific and inapplicable situation.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'I'm out of context again, aren't I?'
jokes.setup: The hotel advice non-sequitur clearly came from somewhere irrelevant
jokes.punchline: The narrator breaks the fourth wall to acknowledge he's been giving advice from a completely different scenario — treating his omniscience as a burden of constant context-switching
jokes.explanation: A beautifully executed meta moment. The AD narrator has always been an implicit presence; having him doubt his own contextual placement is a sophisticated comedy move. 'Again' implies this happens to him regularly — he exists across multiple story lines and occasionally loses track. It's formally inventive and the economy is perfect (seven words). Justifies 8+ for the structural originality of a narrator acknowledging his own unreliability.

jokes.text: Maeby's Mrs. Featherbottom story: '...she meant makeup, not backup person, and then go on to brag that, for four years, she blew three men five times a day'
jokes.setup: Maeby is recounting a chaos situation involving her roommates — someone (apparently in disguise as Mrs. Featherbottom) made an inappropriate cover story
jokes.punchline: 'She blew three men five times a day' — the accidental double entendre used as a bragging point by someone trying to cover a Blue Man Group gaffe
jokes.explanation: The Mrs. Featherbottom callback combined with the Blue Man Group reference (both Tobias running gags) gives this density. The double entendre is functional but the real pleasure is the layered callbacks for longtime viewers.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Oh, yeah. Yeah, we had to do some crazy vote-out thing where he... he forgot how voting worked. He voted for himself.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is advising Maeby on how to vote out difficult roommates
jokes.punchline: He casually recalls Michael voting himself out of his own apartment because he didn't understand the rules — a callback delivered as a cautionary tale
jokes.explanation: Good callback that characterizes Michael's obliviousness without needing to show it. The narrator's following elaboration confirms this is being underplayed for comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'there was something else... afoot.' (while discussing the severed foot threat)
jokes.setup: George Michael explains he kept building the wall after learning there was another reason
jokes.punchline: 'Afoot' is a pun — both meaning 'underfoot / in motion' and literally referring to the severed foot sent by the Chinese investors
jokes.explanation: Classic AD wordplay — 'afoot' doing double duty as idiom and literal body-part reference. The economy is nearly perfect (one word carries the entire joke). George Michael's deliberate or unconscious use of it, and whether Maeby notices, adds another layer of ambiguity.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'You sound just like a slightly self-aware Aunt Lindsay.' / George Michael: 'Well, you sound just like a minimally-woke Uncle Michael.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are arguing about the ethics of building the border wall
jokes.punchline: They insult each other by comparing the other to their respective parents — the ultimate insult for these two characters — with the precise qualifiers ('slightly self-aware,' 'minimally-woke') doing the comedic work
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy with perfect parallelism. 'Slightly self-aware Aunt Lindsay' acknowledges Lindsay has some self-awareness but not enough — it's a precise criticism. 'Minimally-woke Uncle Michael' does the same for Michael's performative but shallow progressivism. These descriptors are also funnier than a direct insult because they imply the comparison itself is insulting. Justifies 8+ because the qualifiers require knowledge of both parent characters and function as micro-character analyses.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Fine. Abandon your morals. Build your dumb wall. Appease your dumb guilt at firing your father because of his dumb investment. It's not dumb. He's the only person who ever believed in this company. Which isn't real, so that makes it dumb.'
jokes.setup: George Michael defends his father's investment in Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Maeby's 'which isn't real, so that makes it dumb' — the company being fake makes emotional investment in it objectively dumb, stated with total logical clarity
jokes.explanation: The callback to Fakeblock not being real demolishes George Michael's sentimental defense with perfect logical economy. Maeby's 'which isn't real, so that makes it dumb' is the correct and devastating response.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'But, hey, maybe you can get those dumb attorneys from that short Netflix show that's not on the air anymore.' / George Michael: 'They canceled that?'
jokes.setup: Maeby is dismissing the Guilty Guys lawyers as an option
jokes.punchline: George Michael asks if the Netflix show was actually canceled — as if he genuinely doesn't know whether the show about his own family's case exists, in a show that is itself on Netflix
jokes.explanation: Second meta-Netflix joke in the episode, this one with George Michael apparently genuinely unaware — layers of meta-commentary about Netflix's content decisions embedded in character dialogue.

jokes.text: I will not let this DA take my job.
jokes.setup: Lucille is arguing to Dusty that she needs him to defend Buster because the DA wants to destroy her son.
jokes.punchline: 'I will not let this DA take my job' — Lucille frames being Buster's overbearing, controlling mother as a 'job' she is professionally protective of.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's possessiveness toward Buster expressed as professional competition is a sharp character moment. 'Take my job' implies she sees dominating Buster as her vocation — which is exactly right. The line reveals character while being genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'I think you're a very caring mother. In fact, I'll go one step further. I think this is how a mother's supposed to act.'
jokes.setup: Lucille argues she's not an overbearing mother for wanting Buster free
jokes.punchline: Dusty sincerely endorses Lucille's parenting as exemplary — which is darkly absurd given that Lucille's mothering is a central source of Buster's criminal dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Functional dramatic irony — endorsing Lucille's parenting as a model is absurd given the narrative — but the joke is too understated to fully land without a stronger setup.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'She's not as sweet as she seems, is she?' / 'Trust me, it's all an act. She's not even interested in children. Well, maybe, like, babies, but that's weird, because who likes babies?' / Lucille: 'Mm, especially in the early years, I know.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is trying to bond with Lucille over their shared contempt for the DA
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually agrees that babies and early childhood are unpleasant — inadvertently confirming she found her own children unpleasant as infants
jokes.explanation: Lucille confirming 'especially in the early years' as her agreement that babies are unpleasant is perfectly in character — she's simultaneously bonding over the DA and revealing she disliked her own children. The 'who likes babies?' question from Dusty is also funny but Lucille's specific endorsement of the sentiment about the 'early years' is the real joke.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'I can't wait to see her face when she learns that you're the one I'm with now. Believe me, I even wanted to lie and tell her things had gotten sexual with us, but... I didn't want to make things weird.'
jokes.setup: Dusty wants to rattle his ex (the DA) by revealing he's with Lucille
jokes.punchline: He wanted to lie and say they'd had sex — but stopped himself because he didn't want to 'make things weird,' implying their current non-sexual relationship is normal but claiming sex would cross a line
jokes.explanation: The inversion of 'what would make things weird' — not the current unusual relationship but the hypothetical sexual one — is a nice cringe beat. Dusty's social calibration is completely off.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'You don't have to tell her about us. I mean, I'm old enough to be your mother.' / Dusty: 'Right. That's why I chose the word weird.'
jokes.setup: Lucille defensively notes she's old enough to be Dusty's mother
jokes.punchline: Dusty confirms this is exactly why he called it 'weird' — validating Lucille's concern in the most socially awkward way possible
jokes.explanation: Clean two-line joke with perfect structure. Lucille's disclaimer is meant to preempt judgment; Dusty's 'right, that's why I chose the word weird' turns it back on her by confirming the relationship is objectively strange. 'That's why I chose the word weird' is quotable and precise — 'chose the word' implies careful vocabulary selection that makes it more formal and funnier.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'But maybe that's why I've been eager to move in with you.'
jokes.setup: Dusty just confirmed the relationship is 'weird' because of the age/mother dynamic
jokes.punchline: He immediately follows this by suggesting the mother-dynamic is WHY he wants to move in with her — confirming an Oedipal subtext that nobody asked him to make explicit
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'that's why it's weird' to 'that's why I want to move in' is a genuinely uncomfortable and funny beat. Dusty's self-analysis makes it worse, not better.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I wouldn't change a thing, and of course, you'll need to cut your hair.' / Dusty: 'Oh, that'll make her totally crazy.' / 'Yeah. She could never get me to do that.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says she accepts Dusty completely and wouldn't change a thing
jokes.punchline: She immediately demands he cut his hair — which his ex-wife could never make him do, meaning Lucille is already more controlling than the ex
jokes.explanation: The setup-punchline structure within a single sentence is classic AD. 'I wouldn't change a thing' → immediate demand for hair change is a perfect misdirection, and the fact that his ex never managed this implies Lucille's controlling nature is more powerful than a failed marriage. Pure Lucille character comedy — she doesn't notice the contradiction. Justifies 8+ for the efficiency and character specificity.

jokes.text: Michael: 'They're gonna do one of their slimy cheat-the-system shortcuts that never works instead of postponing the trial?'
jokes.setup: Michael learns about the Dusty mistrial plan
jokes.punchline: Michael's critique of 'slimy cheat-the-system shortcuts' is delivered by a man who just spent Chinese investors' money without permission and merged companies behind his son's back
jokes.explanation: Michael's total lack of self-awareness is a Series-long joke. He criticizes shortcuts while being the ultimate shortcut-taker. Functional irony but not freshly executed.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I even had to use my show business connections to get a film crew to rent out the cottage for a couple of days... and that was the real payment, just to see her reaction to a boom operator standing on her bed, it was worth it.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains how he's managing financially while Buster's case drains resources
jokes.punchline: His 'real payment' for renting out the family cottage was the look on someone's face when they found a film crew's boom operator on their bed — an inexplicably petty and specific satisfaction
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'boom operator standing on her bed' as a form of petty victory is funny — it's such a precise and absurd grievance. Michael's priorities are characteristically wrong.

jokes.text: Allegedly.
jokes.setup: Michael is discussing something (the context is unclear from the transcript — likely the fraud or the severed foot).
jokes.punchline: 'Allegedly' as a single-word standalone sentence — a classic Bluth legal hedge that has become a running character gag.
jokes.explanation: One word, perfect economy. The Bluths' reflex use of 'allegedly' whenever they say something incriminating is a long-running family tic. It lands here through familiarity and timing rather than new construction.

jokes.text: Michael has been ignoring Lucille 2's calls, believing she disappeared — then realizes the voicemails are all about the same topic and erases them: 'Uh, I guess I erased them. Yeah, fine. Sure, we'll try it your way.'
jokes.setup: Michael claims to have evidence that Lucille 2 was alive after her alleged disappearance — a voicemail that could prove Buster's innocence
jokes.punchline: He erased the voicemails — the key evidence — apparently casually and without consideration, and then immediately caves on the legal strategy
jokes.explanation: Michael destroying key evidence and immediately surrendering to the alternative plan is a great character beat — his incompetence as a hero of the story is a series-long theme. The casual 'uh, I guess I erased them' is perfect deflation.

jokes.text: George Michael trying to get Gob's attention while Gob is listening to music: '♪ Who's gonna tell you when... ♪' / 'Yes? Oh, I just... Yep. Just...'
jokes.setup: George Michael needs to talk to Gob about canceling the wall event
jokes.punchline: Gob is mid-music, takes a moment to shift to the conversation, and his 'Oh, I just... Yep. Just...' suggests he wasn't fully tracking the request at all
jokes.explanation: Minor reaction beat — Gob's distracted nature is well-established, this is a mild execution of a familiar beat. Functional but not distinctive.

jokes.text: Gob: 'If there are gonna be as many people there as you think, I gotta put on a goddamn show. A magic show... from me.'
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests maybe they just cancel the big wall unveiling
jokes.punchline: Gob's response reveals his only thought is whether there will be enough of an audience for his magic show — completely ignoring the legal and financial stakes
jokes.explanation: 'A magic show... from me' with the self-important pause is perfectly Gob. His reframing of a financial/legal crisis as a performance opportunity is pure character comedy.

jokes.text: If there are gonna be as many people there as you think, I gotta put on a goddamn show. A magic show... from me.
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests canceling the public wall event.
jokes.punchline: Gob's instant pivot to 'a magic show... from me' — the self-aggrandizing pause before 'from me' — reveals his narcissism and his inability to see any situation except as a performance opportunity.
jokes.explanation: The pause-and-emphasis on 'from me' is pitch-perfect Gob — as if the magic show's main selling point requires clarification that he will be performing it. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Except for a very few specific moments where we need them to look away, so I can do the little tricky part.'
jokes.setup: Gob is planning to make the wall unveiling into a magic show
jokes.punchline: Gob's magic planning vocabulary — 'the little tricky part' — for what is apparently a significant illusion, delivered with the casual confidence of a professional
jokes.explanation: Gob's magician jargon — 'the little tricky part' for the actual magic — is a nice character detail. His breezy professionalism about the technical elements while being grandiose about the showmanship is funny.

jokes.text: Gob and narrator simultaneously: '...the gay Mafia.' / '...the gay Mafia.'
jokes.setup: Gob is about to explain that the event could be used to lure Tony Wonder out of hiding, but stops himself from mentioning the gay Mafia
jokes.punchline: Both Gob and the narrator say 'the gay Mafia' at exactly the same time — the narrator finishing Gob's unspoken thought while Gob thinks he avoided saying it
jokes.explanation: This is formally exceptional AD comedy writing. The simultaneous narrator/character delivery is a sophisticated technique — usually the narrator editorializes AFTER the character speaks. Here they speak in unison, implying the narrator knows Gob's mind so well he finishes his thought, OR that the narrator is the source of Gob's self-censorship (the concept exists in both their heads). The payoff of the structural trick plus the callback to the gay Mafia running gag makes this a standout moment. Justifies 8+ for formal invention and perfect execution.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Gob would leave the office three minutes later.'
jokes.setup: Gob says 'I'll be here all night!' with great enthusiasm about his plans
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately informs us Gob left three minutes later — directly contradicting his bombastic claim
jokes.explanation: Perfect narrator deployment. The immediate deflation of 'I'll be here all night!' with 'three minutes later' is formally satisfying — the narrator has omniscient knowledge that makes Gob's boast instantly pathetic. The specificity of 'three minutes' rather than 'soon' is funnier.

jokes.text: I thought you wanted me to pre-tie his tie. / He's not here. He ran off. He's flipping out.
jokes.setup: George Sr. arrives at the cottage to review legal strategy and is seemingly holding or wearing something strange.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is there to 'pre-tie his tie' for Dusty — implying Dusty can't tie his own tie, or that George Sr. considers this a legitimate preparatory task.
jokes.explanation: The 'pre-tie his tie' is a minor absurdist detail — George Sr. taking it upon himself to prepare someone else's neckwear is slightly funny. Not a standout joke.

jokes.text: Ah, I hate it! I just wasted an hour and a half working on an opening joke, till I realized that it's from Fast and Furious. It doesn't even work unless you're in a Camaro.
jokes.setup: Dusty is having pre-trial jitters.
jokes.punchline: He spent an hour and a half on an opening joke that he then realized was from Fast and Furious — and the specific limitation 'it doesn't even work unless you're in a Camaro' is the absurdist kicker.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Fast and Furious' and 'doesn't work unless you're in a Camaro' is the joke's strength. A less specific version would be weaker; the Camaro requirement implies this is a very particular Fast and Furious bit that Dusty apparently memorized and only now recognizes as plagiarized. Unexpectedly strong for an aside.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'I just wasted an hour and a half working on an opening joke, till I realized that it's from Fast and Furious. It doesn't even work unless you're in a Camaro.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is panicking about his opening statement for the murder trial
jokes.punchline: He's been working on an 'opening joke' (not an opening argument) that turns out to be plagiarized from Fast and Furious and requires a specific car — simultaneously revealing he's out of practice, confused about courtroom norms, and has been plagiarizing action movie dialogue
jokes.explanation: Excellent comedic writing with multiple simultaneous failures. That Dusty: (1) thinks a murder trial needs an opening joke, (2) spent 90 minutes on it, (3) plagiarized Fast and Furious, and (4) the specific technical complaint that 'it doesn't work unless you're in a Camaro' — each escalation reveals a new layer of dysfunction. 'It doesn't even work unless you're in a Camaro' is the perfect absurdist button. Justifies 8+ for the density of character failure packed into two sentences and the Camaro detail as a genuinely unexpected specific.

jokes.text: Well, because my argument comes right after I state, 'If you think that's funny, wait until you hear what the opening statement the prosecution is gonna give.'
jokes.setup: Lucille asks why Dusty needs an opening joke at all — he should just need an opening argument.
jokes.punchline: Dusty's legal strategy involves a comedy tee-up: his argument follows a line inviting the jury to compare it favorably to the prosecution — treating the courtroom like a comedy club.
jokes.explanation: The comedy club logic applied to a murder trial is inspired absurdism. Dusty is structuring his opening argument like a stand-up set — setting up the prosecution's opening as the weaker act before his headlining argument. The meta quality (a show about comedy including a lawyer who thinks like a comedian) adds resonance.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Why are you worried about a joke? You just need an opening argument.' / Dusty: 'Well, because my argument comes right after I state, If you think that's funny, wait until you hear what the opening statement the prosecution is gonna give.'
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Dusty he just needs an opening argument, not a joke
jokes.punchline: His 'opening argument' IS predicated on the joke — 'if you think that's funny, wait until you hear the prosecution' — which only works if the preceding joke landed, and also requires a very specific logical structure that is not how trials work
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — the revelation that his legal strategy depends entirely on the joke working is absurd but internally coherent. The meta-comedy structure (joke → 'wait until you hear the argument') being misapplied to a murder trial is well-constructed.

jokes.text: You're anxious?! / You're the mother of the defendant, everybody's favorite. / I'm gonna be the one everyone's staring at, and I know literally who's gonna be judging me the most. / The judge can't show favoritism. / Lottie!
jokes.setup: Dusty complains about being anxious about the trial; Lucille points out she's the mother of the defendant.
jokes.punchline: Dusty's fear is about 'who's gonna be judging me the most' — answered with 'the judge' by Lucille, then corrected to 'Lottie!' — his ex-wife, who will be prosecuting while also personally judging him.
jokes.explanation: The misdirect from 'the judge' to 'Lottie' works on a wordplay level — both senses of 'judging.' Competent structure.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Trust me, the moment she sees you walk into that courtroom, the joke's going to be on her!' / Dusty: 'Yeah! That's why I wanted to tell the joke first!'
jokes.setup: Lucille tries to reassure Dusty that his plan to rattle Lottie will work
jokes.punchline: Dusty interprets 'the joke will be on her' literally — he wanted to pre-empt by telling his joke before Lottie can 'be the joke' on him — revealing he is not understanding metaphors
jokes.explanation: The literal/figurative collision is executed cleanly. 'That's why I wanted to tell the joke first!' as a response to 'the joke will be on her' is a precise misunderstanding that compounds his earlier joke obsession.

jokes.text: What are you doing? Nobody's even swimming out there!
jokes.setup: Dusty apparently does something physical — presumably jumps or dives toward an empty pool or ocean.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's baffled 'nobody's even swimming out there' suggests Dusty is attempting to join nonexistent swimmers — a physical/visual joke of a man flailing toward empty water in a tie.
jokes.explanation: The visual of a man in a tie attempting to join phantom swimmers is inherently funny, and Lucille's confused response sells it. The comedy relies on the unseen action, which the transcript implies but doesn't fully convey.

jokes.text: Dusty runs to the beach in his suit and is turned away: 'Hey! Locals only, brosef.' / 'Turista non grata, dude.' / 'What's with the tie?' / 'Yeah, I know. She made me wear it.'
jokes.setup: Dusty runs off to his 'fort' for solace after the argument with Lucille — apparently a beach hangout with surfer friends
jokes.punchline: He's rejected by his own surfer crowd because of the tie Lucille made him wear — Lucille has already domesticated him enough that he can't even access his old life
jokes.explanation: Thematically rich physical comedy. The tie — forced on him by Lucille — physically marks him as domesticated and literally bars him from his surfer community. 'She made me wear it' as his explanation to the surfers who reject him because of it is both sad and funny, and the Latin/slang mix ('turista non grata') from surfers is a nice absurdist detail.

jokes.text: Well, I'm sorry. I'm not trying to be your mother, but... but I'm worried about my son. / Okay. If you don't want to be my mother, then maybe stop referring to me as your son, how about that? / I... I'm talking about Buster.
jokes.setup: Lucille slips into maternal language with Dusty, who objects to being called her son.
jokes.punchline: Lucille clarifies she was talking about Buster — not Dusty — which resolves the miscommunication but also confirms the Oedipal weirdness by showing she was thinking of both simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The Oedipal mix-up is structurally perfect: Dusty's objection ('stop referring to me as your son') is reasonable given the earlier conversation, and Lucille's correction ('I'm talking about Buster') resolves it while confirming the underlying weirdness — she has two sons in the room, one actual and one adopted. The economy of 'I'm talking about Buster' as the punchline is excellent.

jokes.text: George Sr. reminiscing: 'Remember when I had them the night before we went to trial for that tax evasion in '88?' / 'We had to hide all that boy fight money.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is reminding Lucille how they dealt with pre-trial jitters in the past
jokes.punchline: 'All that boy fight money' — delivered as a casual reminder of income they apparently derived from fights involving boys, treated as a mundane financial category
jokes.explanation: The casual 'boy fight money' is a perfect AD callback delivered as throwaway nostalgia. The Season 1 'boyfights' gag is being recalled as a financial instrument hidden during tax evasion proceedings, which is both a meaningful callback and a dark revelation about the Bluth operation. The domestic framing ('remember when') makes the criminal content funnier. Justifies 8+ as a rewarding callback with new information.

jokes.text: Hey! Locals only, brosef. / Turista non grata, dude. / Yo, dudes! It's me, Dusty! / What's with the tie? / Yeah, I know. She made me wear it.
jokes.setup: Dusty retreats from the cottage and apparently tries to join a local surf group.
jokes.punchline: The surfers initially reject him ('locals only, brosef'), then when he identifies himself they accept him — but mock the tie, which he explains with 'She made me wear it' — a completely deflating admission.
jokes.explanation: 'She made me wear it' as the defeated explanation for why the lawyer is in a tie at the beach is a good character beat — it reduces him instantly from a defender of justice to someone who can't refuse his girlfriend's dress code. 'Turista non grata' is also a nice surfer-Latin hybrid.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'You gave me that in case you're going away present. That got the jitters out of me.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. reminisces about how Lucille helped him before the 1988 tax evasion trial
jokes.punchline: The 'in case you're going away present' is a sex act framed as a pre-incarceration gift — delivered with matter-of-fact nostalgia
jokes.explanation: The euphemism 'in case you're going away present' works through implication and the nostalgia framing. Delivered appropriately without spelling out what it is, which is the right call.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'How can you ask me that?' / George Sr.: 'I just know how you can be. There was a time right after Gob was born... when you grew cold and contemptuous of everyone. You remember that?' / Lucille: 'Well, only because it's still going on.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks Lucille to use physical affection to boost Dusty's confidence; she's offended; he reminds her of a post-Gob cold period
jokes.punchline: 'Well, only because it's still going on' — Lucille confirms she never recovered from the post-Gob coldness, meaning she's been cold and contemptuous of everyone for the entire duration of the show
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is impeccable: (1) George Sr. describes a past coldness, (2) asks if she remembers it, (3) Lucille confirms she remembers 'only because it's still going on.' The punchline retroactively confirms that Lucille's entire characterization across the series is a condition she's been aware of and never addressed. It's also a perfect self-aware character admission that isn't really an admission — she's not embarrassed. The economy is extraordinary. Justifies near-9 for structural perfection, total character integration, and the retroactive scope of the punchline across multiple seasons.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Hey, I hate the idea, but we gotta do something to get his confidence in that courtroom, and I am willing to shut my eyes and let it happen.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. suggests Lucille use physical intimacy to help Dusty before the trial
jokes.punchline: 'I am willing to shut my eyes and let it happen' — the husband's noble sacrifice is framed as a personal ordeal he will endure with eyes closed, which is both gallant and pathetic
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s magnanimous eye-closing is a good cringe beat. The framing of 'letting it happen' with closed eyes implies he's conceived of a specific mental strategy for enduring this — which is the right amount of detail for the joke.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Now you know how I felt all those years, George.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. makes his 'shut my eyes' sacrifice speech
jokes.punchline: Lucille reveals that George Sr.'s pained 'let it happen with eyes closed' posture is exactly how she has experienced their own marriage — a devastating marital indictment delivered with perfect timing
jokes.explanation: A companion to joke #69 in quality. The setup is George Sr.'s self-pitying 'shut my eyes' posture; the punchline is Lucille weaponizing it as a description of their entire marriage. The line has to land in one sentence with no qualification, which it does. Like joke #69, it's character-confessional comedy that simultaneously advances character history and is devastatingly funny. It's the correct response to George Sr.'s speech and it's unexpected. Justifies near-9 for perfect structural reversal, total character integration, and high quotability.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'But even though Lucille felt good about making George feel bad, she knew that he was right.'
jokes.setup: After Lucille's devastating comeback
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Lucille's motivation was primarily to 'make George feel bad' — she wasn't making a sincere emotional disclosure, she was running a scoring play — and then immediately notes she accepts his point anyway
jokes.explanation: The narrator's revelation that Lucille's emotional truth was functionally a combat move in the marriage adds a dark layer to the previous devastating joke. 'Felt good about making George feel bad' is a precise and funny description of Lucille's emotional economy.

jokes.text: Lucille arrives at what she imagines is Dusty's modest home — and it turns out to be a mansion with a full multi-floor staff: 'How rich is this sand hobo?'
jokes.setup: Lucille goes to Dusty's home expecting a modest surfer's dwelling; she discovers an enormous estate with butler, multiple staff floors, and elaborate household hierarchy
jokes.punchline: After all the elaborate staff interaction, Lucille's summation is 'How rich is this sand hobo?' — the most Lucille-possible response to discovering her boyfriend is secretly wealthy
jokes.explanation: The sustained comedy of the mansion reveal culminates in the perfect phrase. 'Sand hobo' is a brilliantly specific Lucille Bluth insult — combining her class contempt (hobo) with Dusty's surfer lifestyle (sand). Her first question upon discovering her boyfriend is secretly very rich is not surprise or joy but a practical wealth assessment — which is exactly who Lucille is. Justifies 8+ because 'sand hobo' is a genuinely great coinage and the structure of the scene (elaborate setup → single dismissive phrase) is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: I'm sorry. I thought this was where Dustin Radler lived. / Mr. Radler's in his bedroom. I'll have to announce you. / Announce me?
jokes.setup: Lucille arrives at Dusty's for a 'casual drop-by' and is met by professional staff.
jokes.punchline: 'I'll have to announce you' — the formality of being 'announced' to her own boyfriend undercuts Lucille's casual visit framing completely.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between a 'casual drop-by' and being formally announced sets up the absurd household bureaucracy to follow. Lucille's 'Announce me?' captures her shock at encountering a more elaborate domestic infrastructure than her own.

jokes.text: The household staff argument: First floor vs. second floor hierarchy, Vines, the Salazars, Mrs. Bautista becoming Mrs. Salazar, 'Three guesses what she did to get on two.'
jokes.setup: Lucille arrives at Dusty's mansion and discovers an elaborate servant hierarchy with floor-based status divisions
jokes.punchline: The staff is engaged in their own class war — first floor vs. second floor — with a pantry captain, a datebook requirement for casual drop-bys, and the mystery of how Mrs. Bautista got promoted to the second floor ('three guesses what she did')
jokes.explanation: The absurdist escalation of the servant class system — complete with 'pantry captain,' 'datebook for CDBs,' and unexplained second-floor promotion criteria — is sustained and inventive. The joke is that Dusty has recreated an entire domestic social stratification that mirrors the Bluth family's own class obsessions, and neither Lucille nor the staff seem aware of the irony.

jokes.text: Mrs. Bluth is here for a CDB, and Carla didn't know about it. / Most likely because it wasn't made into a Vine.
jokes.setup: The staff are processing Lucille's unannounced visit ('CDB' = casual drop-by).
jokes.punchline: 'Most likely because it wasn't made into a Vine' — the staff have apparently reduced all scheduling to Vine videos, a deeply absurd and already-dated social media reference.
jokes.explanation: The Vine reference is clever because Vine was already dead by Season 5's production, making it deliberately anachronistic — a rich household stuck on an obsolete social media platform for scheduling. 'CDB' as the staff acronym for 'casual drop-by' is also a nice touch of world-building absurdism.

jokes.text: What, does he now think the Salazars control everything? / I told you! The first floor used to mean something in this house. / Jean, no. / That pantry is unguarded. / You must start keeping up that datebook. Jean!
jokes.setup: The inter-floor staff rivalry has apparently escalated into a full territorial dispute.
jokes.punchline: 'That pantry is unguarded' — the staff are now treating the pantry as contested territory in what appears to be an ongoing power struggle, while Lucille watches the chaos she has inadvertently triggered.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'who handles the CDB' to 'the pantry is unguarded' is inspired. This staff has a full Game-of-Thrones-style domestic politics situation, and Lucille's casual drop-by has exposed it. The absurdist world-building is strong.

jokes.text: Maeby's roommate vote — she loses: 'I'm sorry. I didn't know they would both vote against you.' / 'I voted myself out, too, okay? 'Cause I can't take it anymore.' / 'You've taken all the fun out of the worst part of life.'
jokes.setup: Maeby tries to rig a roommate vote to evict the others
jokes.punchline: The vote goes against Maeby; then she reveals she voted herself out too because she also can't stand living there; 'You've taken all the fun out of the worst part of life' — the worst part of life being bad living situations
jokes.explanation: 'You've taken all the fun out of the worst part of life' is the best line in this sequence — it's a perfectly counterintuitive construction. Bad living situations are terrible but there's a certain resigned commiseration in them; Maeby has apparently made even that miserable. It's also thematically resonant as an AD closing sentiment. Justifies 8+ for the punchline's formal elegance and quotability.

jokes.text: On the next Arrested Development... / Maeby uses her mathematical ingenuity to rig an election.
jokes.setup: The 'on the next' teaser promises Maeby using 'mathematical ingenuity.'
jokes.punchline: Maeby is not known for mathematical ingenuity — the phrase is hilariously incongruous with her character.
jokes.explanation: The incongruity of 'mathematical ingenuity' applied to Maeby is the joke, though it's mild. The teaser format is a show staple.

jokes.text: Maeby finds Sitwell's files: 'So if these people really disappeared... that means there's an ocean view available.'
jokes.setup: Maeby finds Stan Sitwell's files suggesting people have disappeared
jokes.punchline: Her immediate reaction to evidence of possible disappearances/deaths is to calculate real estate opportunity — specifically an ocean view
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-closing beat. The leap from 'missing persons' to 'ocean view availability' in a single sentence is both character-revealing and darkly funny. Maeby's self-interested pragmatism is consistent with her character, and the specificity of 'ocean view' as the value proposition is the right absurd detail. As a closing line it does plot work (teasing future storyline) while being genuinely funny. Justifies 8+ for economy, dark subversion, and perfect character integration.

tmdb_overview: As the trial kicks off, the Guilty Guys throw the family for a loop. Lucille learns surprising news about Dusty. George-Michael plots a distraction.
insight: Season finale pulls 44 jokes but lands below series average—character work carries the weight.
